([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Selección de fútbol de Colombia*), nicknamed ***Los Cafeteros**,* represents [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and is managed by the [Federación Colombiana de Fútbol](/wiki/Colombian_Football_Federation "Colombian Football Federation") (English: Colombian Football Federation), the governing body for [football in Colombia](/wiki/Football_in_Colombia "Football in Colombia"). They are a member of [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL") and are ranked 14th in the [FIFA World Rankings](/wiki/FIFA_World_Rankings "FIFA World Rankings") as of April 2025.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The team are nicknamed *Los Cafeteros* due to the [coffee production in the country](/wiki/Coffee_production_in_Colombia "Coffee production in Colombia"). The national team has been a symbol of nationalism, pride and passion for many Colombians worldwide. Colombia is known for having a passionate fan base, and the team's dances during goal celebrations have been symbolic.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The **Colombia national football team** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Selección de fútbol de Colombia*), nicknamed ***Los Cafeteros**,* represents [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and is managed by the [Federación Colombiana de Fútbol](/wiki/Colombian_Football_Federation "Colombian Football Federation") (English: Colombian Football Federation), the governing body for [football in Colombia](/wiki/Football_in_Colombia "Football in Colombia"). They are a member of [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL") and are ranked 14th in the [FIFA World Rankings](/wiki/FIFA_World_Rankings "FIFA World Rankings") as of April 2025.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The team are nicknamed *Los Cafeteros* due to the [coffee production in the country](/wiki/Coffee_production_in_Colombia "Coffee production in Colombia"). The national team has been a symbol of nationalism, pride and passion for many Colombians worldwide. Colombia is known for having a passionate fan base, and the team's dances during goal celebrations have been symbolic.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The Colombian team has participated in six [FIFA World Cups](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"): [1962](/wiki/1962_FIFA_World_Cup "1962 FIFA World Cup"), [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup"), [1994](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), [1998](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"), [2014](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup") and [2018](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup"). It has also qualified for the upcoming [2026 World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In the 2014 edition held in Brazil, the team achieved its best World Cup performance, reaching the quarter-finals and placing fifth in the final standings.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Its greatest international achievement is winning the [Copa América](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "Copa América") in [2001](/wiki/2001_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2001 Copa América") as hosts, during which the team set a record by winning every match without conceding a single goal. Colombia also finished runner-up in [1975](/wiki/1975_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1975 Copa América") and [2024](/wiki/2024_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2024 Copa América") and finished third five times: in [1987](/wiki/1987_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1987 Copa América"), [1993](/wiki/1993_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1993 Copa América"), [1995](/wiki/1995_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1995 Copa América"), [2016](/wiki/2016_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2016 Copa América"), and [2021](/wiki/2021_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2021 Copa América").

Furthermore, the team managed to make outstanding appearances at the continental level, obtaining from the [Central American and Caribbean Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_Central_American_and_Caribbean_Games "Football at the Central American and Caribbean Games") the gold and bronze medals in [1946](/wiki/Football_at_the_1946_Central_American_and_Caribbean_Games "Football at the 1946 Central American and Caribbean Games") and [1938](/wiki/Football_at_the_1938_Central_American_and_Caribbean_Games "Football at the 1938 Central American and Caribbean Games") respectively,[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Colombia national football team](/wiki/History_of_the_Colombia_national_football_team "History of the Colombia national football team")

### Early history (1900–1946)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history (1900–1946)")]

The development of football in Colombia has long been the subject of scholarly debate. Most historians agree that the [Caribbean Region](/wiki/Caribbean_Region,_Colombia "Caribbean Region, Colombia") served as the primary point of entry for the sport, which is widely believed to have been introduced around 1900 by English railway engineers employed by The Colombia Railways Company.[[14]](#cite_note-champions-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The [Colombian Football Federation](/wiki/Colombian_Football_Federation "Colombian Football Federation") was founded in 1924 under the name Liga de Fútbol and became affiliated with [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL") in 1936.[[14]](#cite_note-champions-14) Colombia played its first international match on 17 February 1926 against [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica_national_football_team "Costa Rica national football team") at the [Estadio Moderno Julio Torres](/wiki/Estadio_Moderno_Julio_Torres "Estadio Moderno Julio Torres") in [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla"), winning 4–1 with a side competing under the name *Selección Atlántico*.[[3]](#cite_note-InternationalDebut-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FirstMatch-4)
In 1937, Colombia formed a national team to compete in the *Juegos del IV Centenario de Cali*, contesting four international matches at the newly inaugurated [Estadio Olímpico Pascual Guerrero](/wiki/Estadio_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Pascual_Guerrero "Estadio Olímpico Pascual Guerrero") against [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico_national_football_team "Mexico national football team"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador_national_football_team "Ecuador national football team"), and [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba_national_football_team "Cuba national football team").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Despite being affiliated with FIFA, Colombia withdrew from the [1938 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup") in [France](/wiki/France "France"), instead participating in the [1938 Central American and Caribbean Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_1938_Central_American_and_Caribbean_Games "Football at the 1938 Central American and Caribbean Games"). During this period, the national team was composed predominantly of players from *Club Juventud Bogotana* (now [Millonarios](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C.")), reflecting the club’s central role in the early development of the national side.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Colombia_en_el_Sudamericano_1945%2C_Estadio%2C_1945-01-26_%2889%29.jpg/250px-Colombia_en_el_Sudamericano_1945%2C_Estadio%2C_1945-01-26_%2889%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colombia_en_el_Sudamericano_1945,_Estadio,_1945-01-26_(89).jpg)

Colombia at their first [South American Championship](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "Copa América") in [1945](/wiki/1945_South_American_Championship "1945 South American Championship")

Colombia made its debut in the [South American Championship](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "Copa América") at the [1945 edition](/wiki/1945_South_American_Championship "1945 South American Championship"), marking the nation’s first participation in continental competition. At the time, Colombian football lacked a professional league and a unified national selection process, so the squad was drawn primarily from [Junior de Barranquilla](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Junior "Atlético Junior"), one of the strongest amateur clubs in the Caribbean region. This decision reflected both practical constraints and a strategy to field an organized, experienced group rather than assembling players from multiple regions. [Roberto Meléndez](/wiki/Roberto_Mel%C3%A9ndez "Roberto Meléndez") served as player‑manager, combining leadership on the field with coaching responsibilities, which was typical in early Colombian football due to the limited availability of formal coaching structures. The team faced established South American powers such as [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team"), Argentina, and [Chile](/wiki/Chile_national_football_team "Chile national football team"), achieving mixed results with notable victories and draws against regional rivals like Ecuador and [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia_national_football_team "Bolivia national football team"). The dominance of Junior players highlighted the fragmented nature of Colombian football governance, in which regional associations often supplied entire squads for national representation. Participation in the tournament exposed Colombian players to higher competitive standards and helped the federation identify areas for structural improvement. The campaign illustrated the challenges of institutional development, including limited resources, regional rivalries, and the absence of a nationwide professional league. Despite these obstacles, the experience established Colombia’s presence on the continental stage and contributed to the nation’s growing football identity. Under Meléndez’s dual leadership, the 1945 Championship symbolised both the ambitions and the organisational potential of Colombian football during the mid-20th century.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Though Colombia withdrew from the [1946 South American Championship](/wiki/1946_South_American_Championship "1946 South American Championship"), later that year the national team secured its first major international title by winning the football tournament at the [1946 Central American and Caribbean Games](/wiki/1946_Central_American_and_Caribbean_Games "1946 Central American and Caribbean Games") held in Barranquilla, Colombia. Competing in a single round‑robin format, Colombia played six matches against regional opponents after Cuba and Mexico withdrew from the football competition before it began, and finished undefeated with 12 points, scoring 20 goals and conceding only seven across the tournament. The team opened the campaign with a 4–2 victory over [Curaçao](/wiki/Cura%C3%A7ao_national_football_team "Curaçao national football team") and followed up with wins against [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela_national_football_team "Venezuela national football team"), [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala_national_football_team "Guatemala national football team"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico_national_football_team "Puerto Rico national football team"), Costa Rica and [Panama](/wiki/Panama_national_football_team "Panama national football team"), demonstrating consistent offensive strength throughout the event.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The football side was led by Peruvian manager [José Arana Cruz](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Arana_(footballer,_born_1902) "José Arana (footballer, born 1902)"), who guided Colombia through all six fixtures, marking an early instance of foreign technical leadership in the national team’s history. Arana Cruz’s tenure in 1946 coincided with the nation’s most successful run in an international competition up to that point, at a time when Colombia’s domestic football infrastructure and international participation were still developing.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Post-war reintegration and first World Cup (1947–1962)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Post-war reintegration and first World Cup (1947–1962)")]

Following their debut at the [1945 South American Championship](/wiki/1945_South_American_Championship "1945 South American Championship"), Colombia’s presence on the international stage became sporadic in the immediate post-war period. Although the national federation gained affiliation with [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in 1936,[[14]](#cite_note-champions-14) the team withdrew from several editions of the [Copa América](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "Copa América") between 1947 and 1957, reflecting ongoing administrative instability and the broader social and economic challenges affecting the country during the mid-20th century.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Colombia_en_el_Sudamericano_1957%2C_Estadio%2C_1957-04-26_%28728%29.jpg/250px-Colombia_en_el_Sudamericano_1957%2C_Estadio%2C_1957-04-26_%28728%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colombia_en_el_Sudamericano_1957,_Estadio,_1957-04-26_(728).jpg)

Colombia at the [South American Championship](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "Copa América") in [1957](/wiki/1957_South_American_Championship "1957 South American Championship")

Colombia returned to the continental championship at the [1957 South American Championship](/wiki/1957_South_American_Championship "1957 South American Championship") in Peru, marking the national team’s re-entry into South American international competition after an extended absence. Prior to 1957, Colombia had participated only sporadically in the tournament, having made their debut at the 1945 edition but withdrawing from several subsequent editions due to administrative instability within the Colombian Football Federation and broader social and economic challenges that affected football development in the country.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Despite finishing near the bottom of the standings, participation in the 1957 championship allowed the Colombian federation to re-establish the team at the continental level, providing players with international experience and laying the groundwork for professionalization and stabilization of football in Colombia, which later contributed to the team's qualification for the [1962 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1962_FIFA_World_Cup "1962 FIFA World Cup").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

This gradual reintegration culminated in Colombia’s qualification for the 1962 FIFA World Cup in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), their first appearance at the finals since their international debut.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Coached by [Efraín Sánchez](/wiki/Efra%C3%ADn_S%C3%A1nchez "Efraín Sánchez"), who also served as team captain, Colombia competed in Group 1 alongside Uruguay, [the Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team"), and [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_national_football_team "Yugoslavia national football team"). Despite finishing bottom of the group, Colombia produced one of the most iconic moments in their football history in their match against the Soviet Union. Trailing 4–1, midfielder [Marcos Coll](/wiki/Marcos_Coll "Marcos Coll") scored directly from a corner kick in what remains the only "gol olímpico" (goal scored directly from a corner) in FIFA World Cup history, beating legendary goalkeeper [Lev Yashin](/wiki/Lev_Yashin "Lev Yashin") in the process.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The tournament represented a symbolic turning point, reaffirming Colombia’s place within global football and setting the stage for more consistent international engagement in the decades that followed.

### First Copa América final and unsuccessful World Cup qualification campaigns (1963–1979)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: First Copa América final and unsuccessful World Cup qualification campaigns (1963–1979)")]

Following their debut at the [1962 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1962_FIFA_World_Cup "1962 FIFA World Cup"), Colombia endured a prolonged period of limited success in international competition. After withdrawing from both editions of the [1959 South American Championship](/wiki/1959_South_American_Championship_(disambiguation) "1959 South American Championship (disambiguation)") held in [Argentina](/wiki/1959_South_American_Championship_(Argentina) "1959 South American Championship (Argentina)") and [Ecuador](/wiki/1959_South_American_Championship_(Ecuador) "1959 South American Championship (Ecuador)"), the team returned to continental competition at the [1963 South American Championship](/wiki/1963_South_American_Championship "1963 South American Championship") in Bolivia, where they finished last with one draw and five defeats.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In qualification for the [1966 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"), Colombia was grouped with Ecuador and Chile, but finished bottom of the section with two points, recording a solitary 2–0 home victory over Chile.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The following year, Colombia again faced Chile in qualification for the [1967 South American Championship](/wiki/1967_South_American_Championship "1967 South American Championship"), but were eliminated after a 5–2 defeat in [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago") and a scoreless draw in [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Colombia next entered qualification for the [1970 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup"), drawn into Group 2 alongside Brazil, [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_national_football_team "Paraguay national football team"), and Venezuela. Despite competitive performances, the team finished third with one win, one draw, and four losses, missing out on qualification.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In preparation for the campaign, Colombia hosted the [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") national team in a pre-World Cup friendly that ended in a 4–0 defeat.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The match was overshadowed by the widely publicized [Bogotá Bracelet](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1_Bracelet "Bogotá Bracelet") incident involving England captain [Bobby Moore](/wiki/Bobby_Moore "Bobby Moore"), which generated significant international attention.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Colombia achieved its most significant international result of the era at the [1975 Copa América](/wiki/1975_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1975 Copa América"). Drawn into Group C with Paraguay and Ecuador, the team won all four matches to advance to the semifinals against Uruguay. A 3–0 home victory and a 1–0 away defeat were sufficient to secure progression on aggregate, sending Colombia to the Copa América final for the first time in its history. In the final, Colombia faced [Peru](/wiki/Peru_national_football_team "Peru national football team") in a three-match series. After each team won its home fixture, the championship was decided by a playoff match held at a neutral venue in [Caracas](/wiki/Caracas "Caracas"). Peru prevailed 1–0, denying Colombia its first continental title.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### 1980s: dawn of the Golden Generation and World Cup qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1980s: dawn of the Golden Generation and World Cup qualification")]

Prior to the 1980s, the Colombia national team was widely regarded as a struggling side, constrained by limited investment from the Colombian Football Federation, inconsistent international participation, and the broader social and political instability affecting the country. Periods of national crisis, including *[La Violencia](/wiki/La_Violencia "La Violencia")* and the rise of organized crime, hindered the development of football infrastructure and delayed the emergence of a sustained footballing culture. Although Colombia had qualified for Chile 1962, the national team largely failed to build on that achievement, earning a reputation as perennial underachievers in international competition.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Colombia_national_football_team_in_1985.jpg/250px-Colombia_national_football_team_in_1985.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colombia_national_football_team_in_1985.jpg)

Colombia in 1985

In the early 1980s, Colombia sought to re-establish itself competitively under Argentine manager [Carlos Bilardo](/wiki/Carlos_Bilardo "Carlos Bilardo"). The team entered the [1982 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "1982 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)") with renewed ambition but failed to secure qualification for the [finals](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup"), and subsequently exited in the first round of the [1983 Copa América](/wiki/1983_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1983 Copa América"), underscoring the persistent gap between promise and results.

Colombia showed tangible progress during qualification for the [1986 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup"). Drawn into [Group 1](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_CONMEBOL_Group_1 "1986 FIFA World Cup qualification – CONMEBOL Group 1") alongside Argentina, Peru, and Venezuela, the campaign began with a notable 1–0 victory over Peru, courtesy of [Miguel Prince](/wiki/Miguel_Augusto_Prince "Miguel Augusto Prince"). Despite suffering defeats to Argentina both at home and away, Colombia recovered with draws and victories against Venezuela to secure a place in the intercontinental play-offs against Paraguay. A 3–0 defeat in [Asunción](/wiki/Asunci%C3%B3n "Asunción") proved decisive, and although Colombia won the return leg 2–1, they were eliminated 4–2 on aggregate, narrowly missing qualification.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Valderrama_maradona_saludo.jpg/250px-Valderrama_maradona_saludo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valderrama_maradona_saludo.jpg)

Colombia and Argentina captains [Carlos Valderrama](/wiki/Carlos_Valderrama "Carlos Valderrama") and [Diego Maradona](/wiki/Diego_Maradona "Diego Maradona") prior to the third-place match at the [1987 Copa América](/wiki/1987_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1987 Copa América")

At the [1987 Copa América](/wiki/1987_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1987 Copa América"), Colombia were drawn into Group C with Bolivia and Paraguay. The team advanced to the semi-finals, where they faced Chile. Colombia briefly took the lead in extra time through a 103rd-minute penalty by [Bernardo Redín](/wiki/Bernardo_Red%C3%ADn "Bernardo Redín"), but Chile responded with two late goals to eliminate Colombia in dramatic fashion. Colombia would go on to win the third-place match against Argentina.

South American qualification for the [1990 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") saw the confederation allocated three-and-a-half places. With Argentina qualifying automatically as reigning champions, the remaining teams were divided into three groups. Colombia emerged as one of the group winners but were required to contest the [CONMEBOL–OFC play-off](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL%E2%80%93OFC_play-off) "1990 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL–OFC play-off)") against [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania_Football_Confederation "Oceania Football Confederation") representatives [Israel](/wiki/Israel_national_football_team "Israel national football team"), who had finished ahead of [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_men%27s_national_football_team "New Zealand men's national football team"). Colombia prevailed 1–0 on aggregate over two legs, securing qualification for their first [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") since 1962 and marking the definitive emergence of a new generation on the international stage.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### 1990s: World Cup return, and the first Colombian Golden Era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1990s: World Cup return, and the first Colombian Golden Era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Selecci%C3%B3n_de_f%C3%BAtbol_de_Colombia%2C_Italia_90.jpg/250px-Selecci%C3%B3n_de_f%C3%BAtbol_de_Colombia%2C_Italia_90.jpg)](/wiki/File:Selecci%C3%B3n_de_f%C3%BAtbol_de_Colombia,_Italia_90.jpg)

Colombia line-up against [West Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") at the [San Siro](/wiki/San_Siro "San Siro") in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), Italy, for the third group-stage fixture of the [1990 World Cup](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup")

At the 1990 World Cup, Colombia was drawn with [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_national_football_team "Yugoslavia national football team"), alongside [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_football_team "United Arab Emirates national football team") and powerhouse [West Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team"). Colombia faced the United Arab Emirates at the [Stadio Renato Dall'Ara](/wiki/Stadio_Renato_Dall%27Ara "Stadio Renato Dall'Ara") in [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), winning 2–0 with goals from Bernardo Redín and Carlos Valderrama to achieve Colombia's first-ever World Cup finals win. Despite losing the second game against Yugoslavia 1–0, Colombia advanced to the round of 16 after a historic 1–1 draw with eventual champions West Germany, where [Freddy Rincón](/wiki/Freddy_Rinc%C3%B3n "Freddy Rincón") equalized in the 93rd minute after West Germany's initial goal five minutes earlier.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Colombia finished the group stage in third place, advancing as the second-best third-placed team. They were eliminated in the round of 16 by [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_national_football_team "Cameroon national football team") with a 2–1 defeat in extra time, after a mistake from Colombian goalkeeper [René Higuita](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Higuita "René Higuita").[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Francisco_Maturana.jpg/250px-Francisco_Maturana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francisco_Maturana.jpg)

[Francisco Maturana](/wiki/Francisco_Maturana "Francisco Maturana") is Colombia's most prominent domestic manager, guiding the national team to the [1990](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_1990 "FIFA World Cup 1990") and [1994](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_1994 "FIFA World Cup 1994") World Cups, and leading them to their first [Copa América](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica_2001 "Copa América 2001") title in 2001.

Shortly after the tournament, Francisco Maturana left his post as manager. He was replaced by [Luis Augusto García](/wiki/Luis_Augusto_Garc%C3%ADa "Luis Augusto García"), who led the team in the [1991 Copa América](/wiki/1991_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1991 Copa América") and interim matches. Maturana returned in 1993 to guide Colombia through the successful 1994 World Cup qualifying campaign.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

With Valderrama as captain, Colombia reached the semifinals of the [1993 Copa América](/wiki/1993_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1993 Copa América"), losing to Argentina on penalties and finishing third after a 2–1 victory over Ecuador.

Colombia finished first in their [1994 World Cup qualifying](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)") group, remaining unbeaten, which included a historic [5–0](/wiki/Argentina_v_Colombia_(1994_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification) "Argentina v Colombia (1994 FIFA World Cup qualification)") victory against Argentina at the [Estadio Monumental](/wiki/Estadio_Monumental_(Buenos_Aires) "Estadio Monumental (Buenos Aires)") in [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Before the [1994 World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), expectations were high. Colombia was drawn in [Group A](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup_Group_A "1994 FIFA World Cup Group A") with hosts [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team"), and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team"). They earned one win over Switzerland, but losses to Romania (3–1) and the United States (2–1, with an own goal by [Andrés Escobar](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Escobar "Andrés Escobar")) led to elimination in the group stage.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

[Hernán Darío Gómez](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Dar%C3%ADo_G%C3%B3mez "Hernán Darío Gómez") (*El Bolillo*) replaced Maturana in 1995. Under his management, Colombia finished third at the [1995 Copa América](/wiki/1995_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1995 Copa América") after defeating the United States 4–1. Two years later, at the [1997 Copa América](/wiki/1997_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1997 Copa América"), they were eliminated in the quarterfinals by hosts Bolivia.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Colombia qualified for the [1998 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup") by finishing third in the [qualifiers](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)") with 28 points. They were drawn in [Group G](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_Group_G "1998 FIFA World Cup Group G") with [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia_national_football_team "Tunisia national football team"), England, and Romania. After a 1–0 defeat to Romania and a 1–0 win over Tunisia, Colombia were eliminated by England (2–0), with a young [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham") scoring his first international goal via a [free kick](/wiki/Free_kick_(association_football) "Free kick (association football)").[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In the [1999 Copa América](/wiki/1999_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1999 Copa América"), Colombia won all their group-stage matches but were eliminated in the quarter-finals after a 3–2 loss to Chile.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### 2000s: First Copa América title and temporary decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000s: First Copa América title and temporary decline")]

In [2000](/wiki/2000_CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "2000 CONCACAF Gold Cup"), Colombia participated in their first [CONCACAF Gold Cup](/wiki/CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "CONCACAF Gold Cup"),[[51]](#cite_note-51) defeating [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_national_football_team "Jamaica national football team") 1–0, losing 2–0 to [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras_national_football_team "Honduras national football team"), and eliminating hosts United States 2–1 on penalties. They beat Peru 2–1 in the semi-finals and lost 2–0 to [Canada](/wiki/Canada_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Canada men's national soccer team") in the final.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Ivan_Cordoba_-_Inter_Mailand_%281%29.jpg/250px-Ivan_Cordoba_-_Inter_Mailand_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ivan_Cordoba_-_Inter_Mailand_(1).jpg)

[Iván Córdoba](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_C%C3%B3rdoba "Iván Córdoba") captained Colombia to their first [Copa América](/wiki/2001_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2001 Copa América") title, scoring the only goal in the final against [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico_national_football_team "Mexico national football team").

Colombia hosted the [2001 tournament](/wiki/2001_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2001 Copa América"), which faced last-minute cancellations and withdrawals due to security concerns.[[52]](#cite_note-footballtimes-52) They opened with wins over Venezuela (2–0), Ecuador (1–0), and Chile (2–0). Colombia then eliminated Peru and Honduras in the quarter-finals and semi-finals, respectively, and defeated Mexico 1–0 in the final with a goal from captain [Iván Córdoba](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_C%C3%B3rdoba "Iván Córdoba"). The team did not concede a single goal and earned the fair play award; goalkeeper [Óscar Córdoba](/wiki/%C3%93scar_C%C3%B3rdoba "Óscar Córdoba") was named best goalkeeper.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Colombia failed to qualify for the [2002 World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"), finishing sixth in the [CONMEBOL qualifiers](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

At the [2003 FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/2003_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2003 FIFA Confederations Cup"), Colombia lost 1–0 to hosts [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"), then beat [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_men%27s_national_football_team "New Zealand men's national football team") and [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team") to reach the semi-finals, losing 1–0 to Cameroon and finishing fourth against [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team").

In the [2004 tournament](/wiki/2004_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2004 Copa América"), Colombia lost to Argentina in the semi-finals, finishing fourth. At the [2005 CONCACAF Gold Cup](/wiki/2005_CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "2005 CONCACAF Gold Cup"), they beat Mexico in the quarter-finals but lost to Panama in the semi-finals.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Colombia narrowly missed the [2006 World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup") by one point. At the [2007 tournament](/wiki/2007_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2007 Copa América"), they finished third in their group with one win, including a 5–0 loss to Paraguay.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

The decade ended with a poor [2010 qualifying](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)") campaign, as constant formation changes and managerial instability, including the replacement of Pinto by [Eduardo Lara](/wiki/Eduardo_Lara "Eduardo Lara") after a 4–0 loss to Chile in September 2008, contributed to Colombia failing to qualify.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### The Pékerman Era: revival and a new Golden Generation (2011–2018)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The Pékerman Era: revival and a new Golden Generation (2011–2018)")]

In the [2011 Copa América](/wiki/2011_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2011 Copa América"), Colombia topped their group but were eliminated 2–0 by Peru in the quarter-finals. The team ended the year ranked 36th in FIFA, up from 54th earlier in the year.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

After hiring [José Pékerman](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_P%C3%A9kerman "José Pékerman") in January 2012,[[65]](#cite_note-65) Colombia achieved a second-place finish in the [FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "FIFA World Cup qualification") CONMEBOL stage with 30 points, qualifying for the [2014 World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup") for the first time since 1998. The team conceded only 12 goals, the second-best defensive record behind Argentina.[[66]](#cite_note-:3-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/James_Rodriguez.jpg/250px-James_Rodriguez.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Rodriguez.jpg)

[James Rodríguez](/wiki/James_Rodr%C3%ADguez "James Rodríguez") won the [Golden Boot](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_awards "FIFA World Cup awards") at Brazil 2014, scoring six goals.

Without injured [Radamel Falcao](/wiki/Radamel_Falcao "Radamel Falcao"), Colombia opened the 2014 World Cup with a 3–0 win over [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team"), followed by a 2–1 victory over [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_national_football_team "Ivory Coast national football team") to top Group C. They defeated Uruguay 2–0 in the round of 16, reaching the quarter-finals for the first time, before losing 2–1 to hosts Brazil. James Rodríguez won the Golden Boot and later the [Puskás Award](/wiki/FIFA_Pusk%C3%A1s_Award "FIFA Puskás Award") for his goal against Uruguay.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

At the [2015 Copa América](/wiki/2015_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2015 Copa América"), Colombia won only against Brazil in the group stage, however, they still managed progress, being eliminated in the quarter-finals by Argentina on penalties. In the [2016 Copa América Centenario](/wiki/2016_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica_Centenario "2016 Copa América Centenario"), they beat United States 2–0 and Paraguay 2–1 to reach the quarter-finals, lost to Costa Rica, then advanced to the semi-finals after defeating Peru on penalties. They finished third by beating hosts United States.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Colombia finished fourth in CONMEBOL to qualify for the [2018 World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup"). They lost 2–1 to Japan, then beat [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team") 3–0 and [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal_national_football_team "Senegal national football team") 1–0 to top Group H. In the round of 16, Colombia drew 1–1 with England and were eliminated 4–3 on penalties.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### 2022 World Cup cycle and the Néstor Lorenzo era (2019–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2022 World Cup cycle and the Néstor Lorenzo era (2019–present)")]

Following the federation's choice to not renew Pekerman's contract, former [Iran](/wiki/Iran_national_football_team "Iran national football team") manager [Carlos Queiroz](/wiki/Carlos_Queiroz "Carlos Queiroz") was hired to coach the national team.

Starting off their [2019 Copa América](/wiki/2019_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2019 Copa América") campaign, Colombia defeated Argentina 2–0, marking their first victory over the *La Albiceleste* since 2007.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Days later, they faced [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar_national_football_team "Qatar national football team") and won 1–0 to advance to the next round. They finished the group stage with a 1–0 victory over Paraguay, ending with nine points, four goals scored, and none conceded.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) Colombia also became the only team since the [2001 edition](/wiki/2001_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2001 Copa América") to advance out of the group stage with a 100% record.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Despite this, Colombia were eliminated by Chile in a penalty shootout in the quarter-finals.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Colombia opened the [2022 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)") with victories over Venezuela and Chile. However, following the resumption of matches after the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the team's form deteriorated, culminating in their heaviest home defeat in 82 years against Uruguay and a 6–1 loss to Ecuador. The downturn brought an end to Carlos Queiroz's tenure in December 2020. On 14 January 2021, the Colombian Football Federation announced the return of Reinaldo Rueda as head coach.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/David_Ospina%2C_2015-05-31.JPG/250px-David_Ospina%2C_2015-05-31.JPG)](/wiki/File:David_Ospina,_2015-05-31.JPG)

Goalkeeper [David Ospina](/wiki/David_Ospina "David Ospina") became Colombia's most capped player in history during Colombia's quarter-final victory against Uruguay at the [2021 Copa América](/wiki/2021_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2021 Copa América"), reaching 112 appearances.

In the [2021 Copa América](/wiki/2021_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2021 Copa América"), Colombia won their opening match against Ecuador, then drew 0–0 with Venezuela.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) They lost 2–1 to Peru in their third match. Next, Colombia lost to Brazil but progressed to the quarter-finals and then to the semi-finals after defeating Uruguay on penalties following a 0–0 draw.[[81]](#cite_note-81) In the semi-finals, Colombia drew 1–1 with Argentina before losing on penalties.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Colombia defeated Peru 3–2 in the third-place match, with [Luis Díaz](/wiki/Luis_D%C3%ADaz_(footballer,_born_1997) "Luis Díaz (footballer, born 1997)") scoring twice, making him the top scorer alongside [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi").[[83]](#cite_note-83)

After the Copa América, Colombia resumed World Cup qualification. The team went seven matches without scoring, but defeated Venezuela 1–0 in their final match. Results elsewhere left Colombia in sixth place, missing out on the World Cup. Rueda left shortly after.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

On 2 June 2022, the Colombian Football Federation appointed [Néstor Lorenzo](/wiki/N%C3%A9stor_Lorenzo "Néstor Lorenzo") as head coach for a four-year term.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

On 28 February 2023, the federation launched "Futbol con Futuro" (Football with a Future), a project supported by FIFA covering 2022–2025, aiming to reinforce all national teams’ structures and achieve success on the pitch.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Under Lorenzo, Colombia compiled a 28-match unbeaten run leading up to the [2024 Copa América](/wiki/2024_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2024 Copa América") final, which they lost to Argentina in extra time on 14 July 2024. This run included victories over Germany, Brazil, and [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team").[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In September 2025, Colombia qualified for the 2026 FIFA World Cup, their seventh World Cup appearance, after finishing third in CONMEBOL qualifying.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Venezuela

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Venezuela")]

Colombia's main regional football rival has long been neighbouring [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela_national_football_team "Venezuela national football team"). Although Colombia has historically dominated head‑to‑head meetings—with more wins, goals and overall victories—the rivalry has seen notable moments that reflect the improving quality of Venezuelan football since the late 1990s, when the sport began to overtake baseball in popularity in Venezuela.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90) One of the most significant upsets occurred in the [2006 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)"), when Venezuela defeated Colombia 1–0 in Barranquilla, a result widely regarded as a watershed moment for the Vinotinto.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

During the 2026 World Cup qualifying cycle, Colombia again asserted its superiority over Venezuela, first winning 1–0 in the early stages of the closing campaign,[[92]](#cite_note-92) and later securing a decisive 6–3 victory in Maturín that extinguished Venezuela’s hopes of reaching the intercontinental playoff, effectively eliminating them from contention for their first ever World Cup finals.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Argentina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Argentina")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Argentina_-_Colombia_2022_%2857%29.jpg/250px-Argentina_-_Colombia_2022_%2857%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Argentina_-_Colombia_2022_(57).jpg)

Colombia and Argentina players dispute a play during a [2022 World Cup qualifier](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)") on 1 February 2022.

The rivalry between Colombia and the [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") is rooted in a long competitive history and significant matches, often drawing substantial attention in South America. Argentina holds the overall edge in head‑to‑head meetings, but Colombia has registered several memorable results.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

One of the most iconic matches in the rivalry took place on 5 September 1993, when Colombia defeated Argentina 5–0 in Buenos Aires during the [1994 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)"), marking Argentina’s first home defeat in a World Cup qualifying match at the [Estadio Monumental](/wiki/Estadio_Monumental_Antonio_Vespucio_Liberti "Estadio Monumental Antonio Vespucio Liberti").[[95]](#cite_note-95) Colombia also beat Argentina 3–0 in the group stage of the [1999 Copa América](/wiki/1999_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1999 Copa América"), a game remembered for Argentine striker [Martín Palermo](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_Palermo "Martín Palermo") missing three penalties.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

At the [2021 Copa América](/wiki/2021_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2021 Copa América"), Argentina and Colombia met in the semi‑final in [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília"). After a 1–1 draw in regular time, the contest went to a penalty shootout, which Argentina won 3–2 to advance to the final. The shootout included a widely‑reported flurry of “gamesmanship” and taunting. [Emiliano Martínez](/wiki/Emiliano_Mart%C3%ADnez "Emiliano Martínez") was heard attempting to distract Colombia’s takers before their kicks, and after [Yerry Mina](/wiki/Yerry_Mina "Yerry Mina")’s penalty was saved, [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") — who had played alongside Mina at [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") — was caught on camera shouting “¡bailá ahora!” (“Dance now!”) in Mina’s direction, a clear reference to Mina’s exuberant celebration after scoring in Colombia’s previous shootout win over Uruguay earlier in the tournament.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)

In the [2024 Copa América final](/wiki/2024_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica_final "2024 Copa América final"), Argentina defeated Colombia 1–0 after extra time to win the championship, with [Lautaro Martínez](/wiki/Lautaro_Mart%C3%ADnez "Lautaro Martínez") scoring the decisive goal at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Following the match, Argentine players were widely reported to have sung and chanted references to “El Ritmo Que Nos Une” — a song associated with Colombia’s 2024 campaign — during their trophy celebrations, an incident that drew significant media coverage and reaction from fans of both teams.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

In June 2025, during a 2026 World Cup qualifier at the Estadio Monumental in Buenos Aires, the sides played out a 1–1 draw. During the match, a tense on‑field exchange occurred between Argentine captain [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") and Colombian captain [James Rodríguez](/wiki/James_Rodr%C3%ADguez "James Rodríguez") after a stoppage in play. Messi confronted Rodríguez about comments he had made earlier in the year regarding the [2024 Copa América final](/wiki/2024_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica_final "2024 Copa América final"), in which Argentina beat Colombia 1–0, accusing him of saying that the referees had helped Argentina in that match and saying “you talk too much”, to which Rodríguez replied that he “didn’t say anything”.[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103) The confrontation highlighted lingering tensions from that tournament, as Rodríguez had publicly suggested that external factors, including refereeing decisions, played a role in Argentina’s victory in the final.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

Despite these intense moments, the Colombia–Argentina rivalry is often described in media and commentary as one based on competitive respect between two of South America’s most prominent footballing nations, with each meeting adding new layers to their shared history on the pitch.

## Home stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Home stadium")]

Main article: [Estadio Metropolitano Roberto Meléndez](/wiki/Estadio_Metropolitano_Roberto_Mel%C3%A9ndez "Estadio Metropolitano Roberto Meléndez")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Estadio_Metropolitano_baq.jpg/250px-Estadio_Metropolitano_baq.jpg)](/wiki/File:Estadio_Metropolitano_baq.jpg)

Recently, the [Estadio Metropolitano Roberto Meléndez](/wiki/Estadio_Metropolitano_Roberto_Mel%C3%A9ndez "Estadio Metropolitano Roberto Meléndez") has been Colombia's most frequent venue for home matches.

Colombia plays their qualifying matches and friendlies at the [Estadio Metropolitano Roberto Meléndez](/wiki/Estadio_Metropolitano_Roberto_Mel%C3%A9ndez "Estadio Metropolitano Roberto Meléndez") in [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla"), which is the home stadium of the local football team [Atlético Junior](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Junior "Atlético Junior"). The stadium is named after former Colombia national team player [Roberto Meléndez](/wiki/Roberto_Mel%C3%A9ndez "Roberto Meléndez"), who is widely considered to be Colombia's best player during Colombia's amateur era in the 1930s and 1940s. The stadium was used for all of Colombia's successful World Cup qualifying campaigns since 1990.

The [Estadio Nemesio Camacho El Campín](/wiki/Estadio_El_Camp%C3%ADn "Estadio El Campín") in the capital city of [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá") acts as the national team's alternative stadium. The stadium is the playing ground of local clubs [Millonarios](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C.") and [Santa Fe](/wiki/Independiente_Santa_Fe "Independiente Santa Fe"), and hosted Colombia's [2001 Copa América final](/wiki/2001_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica_final "2001 Copa América final") win against Mexico.

## Team image

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Colombia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Team image")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/40px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg)

Wikimedia Commons has media related to [Colombia national football team kits](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Colombia_national_football_team_kits "commons:Colombia national football team kits").

Traditionally, Colombia's home colours are yellow shirts with navy trim and navy or white shorts and socks, with their away colours being normally navy shirts. They wore their first ever red kit at the [2014 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup"). Colombia used red as their home colours in the 20th century, although in [Copa América Centenario](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica_Centenario "Copa América Centenario") the team played in an all-white kit for the first time in their history, before reverting to the yellow and navy kit thereafter.
